title: The Art of War
author: Sunzi, active 6th century B.C.
summary: Sun Tzŭ’s The Art of War is a concise ancient Chinese treatise on military strategy and command, preserved with commentary and scholarly notes. The text presents principles of leadership, discipline, planning, deception, terrain, maneuver, and use of spies, illustrated by anecdotes such as Sun Tzŭ drilling the King of Wu’s concubines and executing disobedient officers to enforce obedience. Lionel Giles provides a meticulous translation, textual concordance, and critical apparatus, discussing earlier corrupt translations, French and Japanese receptions, and native commentators. The work emphasizes clear orders, hierarchical responsibility, economy of force, strategic flexibility, and the moral and practical limits of war.

characters.name: Sun Tzŭ (Sun Wu)
characters.personality: disciplined, pragmatic, authoritative teacher and strategist
characters.lifespan: historical author of the treatise; in the anecdote he commands drills and enforces discipline

characters.name: Ho Lu (King of Wu)
characters.personality: curious, indulgent monarch who tests and then doubts the severity of military discipline
characters.lifespan: appears in anecdote as the sovereign who commissions Sun Tzŭ and intervenes to spare concubines

characters.name: 180 palace concubines (group)
characters.personality: initially amused and undisciplined; then trained under Sun Tzŭ's drill
characters.lifespan: drilled as part of Sun Tzŭ's demonstration; serve as illustration of discipline and command

characters.name: Two company leaders (unnamed)
characters.personality: appointed officers whose failure to secure obedience marks them as responsible
characters.lifespan: executed by Sun Tzŭ in the anecdote for failure to maintain discipline

characters.name: The next-in-order pair (unnamed)
characters.lifespan: installed immediately and lead the companies successfully in drill

characters.name: Ssu-ma Ch’ien
characters.personality: historian and biographer; cited as source for Sun Tzŭ's life
characters.lifespan: quoted in the Introduction as the provider of Sun Tzŭ's biography

characters.name: Lionel Giles
characters.personality: scholarly, exacting, critical of bad translations, meticulous annotator
characters.lifespan: translator and annotator of the edition; provides preface, notes, concordance and criticism

characters.name: Captain Valentine Giles
characters.lifespan: named in the dedication as Lionel Giles's brother; no further fate in text

characters.name: Joseph Amiot (Père Amiot)
characters.personality: early European translator (Jesuit) whose French rendering is criticized
characters.lifespan: authored an imperfect French translation; quoted and critiqued in Giles's preface

characters.name: Capt. E. F. Calthrop
characters.personality: military translator whose English edition is judged unsatisfactory
characters.lifespan: published early English translations (Tokyo, revised editions) criticized by Giles

characters.name: Bob Sutton
characters.personality: modern editor/convertor of the Project Gutenberg etext
characters.lifespan: prefaces the Project Gutenberg Etext and notes conversion decisions

characters.name: Sun Hsing-yen
characters.personality: editor/commentator mentioned by Giles for paragraph divisions
characters.lifespan: cited as an editor whose paragraphing Giles broadly follows

characters.name: Legge
characters.personality: scholar cited as exemplar for concordance method
characters.lifespan: referenced by Giles for the concordance approach

characters.name: Samuel B. Griffith
characters.personality: later translator noted as more lucid but less annotated
characters.lifespan: mentioned as a later translator whose work complements Giles's edition

characters.name: Shanghai critic (unnamed)
characters.lifespan: mentioned hypothetically as a severe reviewer who dislikes translations

characters.name: George (from The Vicar of Wakefield)
characters.personality: literary figure used by Giles as a comparison in a rhetorical flourish
characters.lifespan: referenced by Giles in a rhetorical aside about paradoxes

locations.name: Ch'i (ancient State of Qi, capital Linzi area)
